content.text: npm (Node Package Manager) is the default package manager bundled with Node.js. It provides three things: a CLI for installing and managing packages, a public registry (registry.npmjs.org) hosting over two million packages, and a package metadata format (package.json). Angular projects rely on npm for installing the Angular CLI, framework libraries, testing tools, and build dependencies.
Every Node.js project starts with a
file. It records the project name, version, scripts, and dependency version ranges. Angular CLI generates one automatically with every new workspace.
Angular libraries declare their Angular peer

# Install all dependencies from package.json (clean install uses lock file)
npm install           # short: npm i
npm ci                # preferred in CI — strictly follows package-lock.json

# Add a new runtime dependency
npm install @angular/material@18

# Add a dev dependency
npm install --save-dev @types/node

# Install globally (CLI tools only — never Angular framework packages)
npm install -g @angular/cli

# See outdated packages
npm outdated

# Update a specific package within its allowed range
npm update rxjs

# Interactive upgrade tool (beyond range, handles peer deps)
npx npm-check-updates -u && npm install

# Remove a package
npm uninstall lodash

# Clean-reinstall node_modules (useful when lockfile drifts)
rm -rf node_modules && npm ci

# Check for known vulnerabilities
npm audit

# Auto-fix low/moderate severity issues
npm audit fix

# Detailed report including why each package is included
npm audit --json | jq '.vulnerabilities | keys'
. A breaking change increments MAJOR, a backwards-compatible feature increments MINOR, and a bug-fix increments PATCH.
for most packages (allows minor updates) and
pins the exact installed version so every developer and CI job gets the same tree regardless of the range.
The lockfile records the exact version, resolved URL, and integrity hash of every package in the dependency tree — including transitive dependencies. Never edit it by hand.
Update one package and regenerate lock
It guarantees reproducible builds. Checking it in is the single most effective way to prevent the 'works on my machine but breaks in CI' class of failures.
field turns npm into a simple task runner. Angular CLI hooks its commands into
, but you can add your own.
hooks that run automatically before/after a named script.

npm run build           # run the "build" script
npm run start:prod      # colons are valid in script names
npm test                # "test", "start", "stop" skip the "run" keyword
npm run analyze -- --port 9000   # pass extra args after --

npm 7+ supports workspaces for managing multiple packages in a single repository — the pattern Angular uses for its own source and which large enterprise Angular projects often adopt via Nx or Angular multi-project workspaces.

# Install all workspace packages
npm install

# Run a script in every workspace
npm run test --workspaces

# Run only in one workspace
npm run build --workspace=packages/my-lib

# Add a dependency to one workspace
npm install lodash --workspace=apps/admin
Using npx to Run CLI Tools
runs a package binary without installing it globally — useful for running the Angular CLI in projects where the version is pinned locally.

# Use the locally installed CLI (preferred — avoids version mismatch)
npx ng generate component header

# Same as above via package.json scripts
npm run ng -- generate component header
When a transitive dependency has a security issue and a direct patch is not yet available, npm
force a specific version for any nested consumer:
Common npm Pitfalls in Angular Projects
Pick one tool; delete the other's lockfile
Use local CLI via npx or npm run ng
npm install the required peer explicitly
Add node_modules to .gitignore; use npm ci
npm install in CI instead of npm ci
Always use npm ci in CI pipelines
If you extract a shared Angular library, you can publish it to npm or a private registry. Publishing requires an npm account and a correctly configured package.json.

npm login                               # authenticate once
ng build my-lib --configuration production
npm publish --dry-run                   # preview what would upload
npm publish                             # publish to registry
npm publish --tag beta                  # pre-release tag (skips 'latest')
npm version patch                       # bump 1.0.0 -> 1.0.1 + git tag
Enterprise projects proxy the public registry through an internal one for caching and license control. Configure the registry per-scope in .npmrc.
Never commit auth tokens directly into .npmrc. Use environment variable interpolation (
) or npm login, which writes a per-user ~/.npmrc automatically.
npm and Angular CLI: How They Interact
The Angular CLI is itself an npm package. Every ng command invokes Node.js processes, resolves packages from node_modules, and uses your package.json scripts.
is the recommended way to upgrade Angular packages. It goes beyond bumping versions — it runs migration schematics that rewrite code to match the new API.

ng update                              # see available updates
ng update @angular/core @angular/cli   # always upgrade these together
ng update @angular/material
ng update @angular/core --force        # bypass peer dep check (caution)
combines npm install with a setup schematic that wires the package into your Angular workspace automatically.

ng add @angular/material   # installs + configures theme + animations
ng add @angular/pwa        # adds service worker and manifest
ng add @angular/ssr        # sets up server-side rendering

npm list --depth=0         # all top-level dependencies
npm why rxjs               # why is rxjs installed?
npm list rxjs              # all versions of rxjs in the tree
npm info @angular/core dist-tags  # versions on registry
npm dedupe                 # flatten duplicates where semver allows
. Understanding the cache helps diagnose corrupt installs and speeds up CI.

npm config get cache       # view cache location (~/.npm)
npm cache clean --force    # delete cache (fixes corrupt installs)
npm install --prefer-offline  # use cache; download only if missing
npm ci                     # fastest: skips resolution, uses lockfile
Cache-first; fails only if not cached
Deletes cache; next install re-downloads all
npm scripts can read and validate environment variables before the Angular build runs. A common pattern is a prebuild hook that fails fast if required vars are missing.
Setting env vars inline differs by OS. Use the
npm 7+ workspaces let you manage multiple packages in one repository — the pattern Nx and Angular multi-project workspaces adopt.

npm install                         # install all workspaces
npm run test --workspaces            # run test in every workspace
npm run build --workspace=packages/my-lib   # one workspace only
npm install lodash --workspace=apps/admin   # add dep to one workspace
checks installed packages against a database of known vulnerabilities (CVEs). Run it regularly as part of CI.

npm audit                    # show all vulnerabilities
npm audit --audit-level=high  # fail CI only on high/critical
npm audit fix                  # auto-fix safe upgrades
npm audit fix --force          # upgrade breaking changes (test first)
npm audit --json | jq '.metadata.vulnerabilities'  # parse programmatically
When a transitive dependency cannot be upgraded, use the
field in package.json to force a specific version for any consumer in the tree:
Continuous integration pipelines need deterministic, fast, and secure package installs. The following practices apply to GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and Azure Pipelines alike.

# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name: CI

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Setup Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
          cache: 'npm'       # caches ~/.npm between runs

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci           # never npm install in CI

      - name: Lint
        run: npm run lint

      - name: Test
        run: npm test -- --no-watch --no-progress --browsers=ChromeHeadless

      - name: Build
        run: npm run build -- --configuration production
Installs exactly what the lockfile specifies — no surprises
Shaves 30-60 seconds per run after the first cold install
Masks version conflicts that will break at runtime
Avoids subtle behaviour differences across Node major versions
Common npm Pitfalls in Angular Projects
Choose one tool; delete the other lockfile
Use local CLI via npx or npm run ng
Add to .gitignore; use npm ci in CI
Always use npm ci in CI
npm is the dependency management layer that every Angular project depends on. Key takeaways:
